text: The patient is positioned laterally, with a camera port inserted at the umbilicus. Two robotic 8mm ports are placed at the midclavicular and anterior axillary lines, while a 5mm port is positioned for the assistant. A 12mm port is used for the liver retractor.

The liver is mobilized cephalically using a liver retractor. Laparoscopic intraoperative ultrasound is utilized to guide the dissection of the adrenal vein near the inferior vena cava (IVC) and adrenal gland, aiding in precise robotic dissection.

The inferior vena cava (IVC) is visible, with the retroperitoneal covering being incised adjacent to it. Despite the limited space between the adrenal gland and the IVC, the camera magnification facilitates precise dissection without the need for direct retraction of the IVC.
The accessory adrenal vein is visualized and dissected using a harmonic scalpel. The superior pedicle, originating from the diaphragmatic branch, is controlled with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the inferior pedicle originating from the superior polar branch of the renal artery. The inferior lateral attachments are carefully dissected and divided.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic shear to selectively dissect the accessory adrenal veins, achieving excellent hemostasis. The final dissection of the adrenal vein is shown at its junction with the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic shear to safely divide the adrenal vein, and then places the excised specimen into a catch bag.
The total console time was eighteen minutes. The total operative time was forty-nine minutes.

The patient was discharged the next day. The surgery was carried out without the use of clips or sutures. The use of the ultrasonic shears for the sealing of vessels during robotic assisted adrenalectomy is safe and effective. Based on our initial experience, newer energy devices such as the ultrasonic shears are useful tools that facilitate safe adrenal surgery.
